preFix.title: Composting manure from small animals: rabbits, guinea pigs, and chickens
preFix.subtitle: How to hot-compost pet and livestock manure safely for the garden
preFix.excerpt: Rabbit and guinea pig droppings are cold manures, low in nitrogen relative to chicken manure, with a carbon-to-nitrogen ratio around 25:1, which means they can go into a compost heap without burning plants. Chicken manure is hot, up to 40:1 nitrogen by dry weight, and must be diluted or cold-composted before use. Knowing the difference prevents scorched seedlings and wasted compost.
preFix.sourceNotes: RHS composting guidance for animal manures. ADAS Nutrient Management Guide (RB209) for nitrogen content of manures. US Cooperative Extension Service on backyard composting of manures.

content.text: Rabbit and guinea pig droppings are one of the best garden inputs available to a household with small pets. They are drier than chicken manure, lower in nitrogen per gram, and composed mostly of partially digested fibre that breaks down quickly. Fresh rabbit pellets can go directly around established plants as a top dressing, or into a compost heap as a nitrogen activator. Guinea pig droppings, being similar in composition and often mixed with bedding straw, behave identically.
Add directly to the compost heap with no composting required. Mix with the existing materials rather than dumping in a pile. The
of fresh pellets (around 10 to 15:1) is in the nitrogen-rich range, so pair with card or straw.
Hay and straw bedding from the cage is an excellent brown. Add all of it with the droppings, the straw bulks the heap and improves aeration.
Paper-based bedding (shredded newspaper, cardboard strips) composts well with the droppings.

Chicken manure has a nitrogen concentration of 1.0 to 1.5% by wet weight, roughly twice that of rabbit droppings. Applied fresh in quantity around plants, it can release ammonia and scorch roots and foliage. It needs composting first:
Add the coop cleanout (droppings + straw bedding) to the heap in layers, alternating with equal volumes of carbon material (straw, card, autumn leaves).
If hot composting, a chicken-manure heap will heat readily to 55 to 65°C. Turn it every 3 to 5 days and monitor with a
. Reaching 55°C for 15 days (turning 5 times) meets the standard threshold for pathogen reduction in a hot heap.
If cold composting, allow 6 to 12 months before use on edible crops. The material is ready when it smells earthy and the original bedding is no longer visible.
Cat and dog waste: high risk of Toxocara and other parasites. Not safe for home composting where edible crops will grow.
Hamster and gerbil bedding with sawdust from treated wood, the biocides in treated wood shavings inhibit microbial activity. If the bedding is untreated wood shavings, hay, or paper, it is fine.
